author: @Mrnichols92
text:
i have absolutely zero knowledge about any of this stuff so mind me if my question is stupid... could you not design the blades to funnel the air up into the Centre of the turbine and out through the top? to my mind, if air goes in from any angle, then air must alos need to flow back out, and wouldnt this be counterintuitive to the air that is trying to get in from other angles? in effect, slowing down the turbine? i am probably totally wrong.

but i thought if every blade was shaped to direct the air that pass them, in and upwards and out of a hole in the top, then the outflow of air will not be competing against the in flow that you want for generating energy.

author: @moniqueveenstra8274
text:
As JP Later also reacted here, an external (square) frame (welded) with also a bearing at the top is the solution against the rocking of the reel and without the drag. I would opt for small car rear axles complete with the wheel bearings and (the lightened) brake drums acting as a flange. The whole contraption can then be aligned with slotted holes in the external frame. Of course, the frame is equipped with adjustable feet and weighted with concrete tiles that also fit exactly at the bottom of the frame.
Furthermore, I would cover half of the vertical wind-catching reel with a semicircular vertical rotating hood (sheet aluminium?) in an extra lighter extra frame so that half of the reel does not have to turn towards  the resisting wind. There must therefore also be bearings above and below the frame to optimally position that semicircular hood in the wind with a large fane (also sheet aluminium).

author: @rubartknas7075
text:
Living in a coastal area of northern germany ,where the wind at this time of year is often ruff , wet and pregnant with salt and even "debris", lets me ponder over these  casters. They may not roll as smoothely as in "laboratory conditions" all the time and  the friction may thus increase over time especially if some leaves or other stuff the wind carries make their way to the surface the casters roll on. I would give the "bottom top-plate and bearing construction one more try . Instead of baffles or straight planks why not use some (circular diameter) drain pipe, maybe with threaded rods on the inside to pressurize top and bottom like "0815419" suggested below, but with the bigger diameter of the drainpipe to gain contact face/stability  .  As each lighthouse in coastal areas standing strong against the winds for centuries teaches us, that behind "round corners" the air flow and pressure is almost as high  as in front of it, nomatter which direction the wind blows, these plastic pipe columns could be a useful choice as they are almost invisible to the wind.
Installed at max. possible distance to the rotation center , as far as the bottom plate allows, the airflow behind the columns gets the chance to straighten out/  possible mini turbulences may vanish after the "convergence zone" at the immediate proximity of the columns. Keep your inspiring work up...

author: @GoVertical17
text: No wind, no power. Gravity is always on.

i would get rid of the wheels, use 4 threaded rods to fix the exact position in hight and use wire, in a x-configuration,  to get the top and bottom in a stable position. 
and remember, the salvonius wind "turbine" (that is the name of that typ of thing)  is calculated different from propeller turbines.  probs uses the whole blade surface to collect force. 
a propeller tip spinns with the same speed as the wind goes. 
a salvonius always has half of the surface moving against the wind and therefore it is slower, but nicer, more quiet and no shadow throwing that makes the neighbor crazy.  
:)

thumbs up - nice work as always !!!!

author: @AzureSky6612
text:
Hi Robert, I’ve watched your series on making your wind turbine.  I like it.  On the electrical generation side of things, I noticed some of your comments and issue(s) you pointed out.  Here are my responses and suggestions:
#1] The issue with the turbine ‘locking up’ when you used more than 1 microwave oven transformer coil and your solution of a bridge rectifier and capacitor:  The reason this is happening is due to the fact that: With 2 (or more) coils you absolutely must get them synchronised.  In the alternating current sine wave, the peaks and troughs for each coil MUST align.  If, for instance, in one time the one coil is outputting a positive sine wave peak and the other is simultaneously outputting a negative (trough), then the two will cancel out each other, the result being the turbine being very difficult to turn.  It is imperative to synchronise the coils for if they are even slightly out you will get a power loss.  Not only must the coils be aligned, ideally all the magnets need to be precisely spaced and placed else you will get a power loss when using multiple coils.  The other solution is to use only 1 powerful coil and ditch the multi-coil idea.  You could, for instance use powerful magnets and get them as close as possible to a powerful high-turn-winding coil with a good core.  Doing so would eliminate any need for synchronisation and accurate magnet.  Alternatively, as you are doing, you could place bridge rectifiers and capacitors on each of your coils and then deal purely with a DC output.  Of course, the diodes will drop your voltage and you will incur some power loss due to the circuitry.  If you want the output to remain AC, like a typical generator, of course, then you’d need to consider the aforementioned.
#2] You point out some of your observations about using the microwave oven transformer core/body vs not using it, and that it works better with the core as opposed to only the coil:  There are a couple of reasons for this, the main one being: With the core present it forms, in and around the coil, a type of coherency as is the case with a single magnetic field; in particular, it unifies the magnetic field such that it is centred in the centre of the coil.  Without the core, the coil (edges) acts rather more like individual elements (and less as a single stator ‘entity’).  Having a single core element (as with the spindles and bolt) should work better than the microwave oven transformer core/body.
#3] Size of the coils and/or coil core(s) : This will also make a difference, since you do not want the core(s) to be larger than the magnets and their spacing.  In doing so you will incur power losses, especially when NOT using any cores (just plain coils).  The microwave oven transformer cores are likely too big for the magnets you are using and your idea of spindles with bolts (softer metals such as plain iron work better) is a better (more efficient) approach since the cores are not larger than the magnets, yet can be scaled up all the way to the same size as your magnets (for optimal power generation).

author: @MiscDotGeek
text:
I love seeing the evolution of the design. I really do think that it'll be better without the supports blocking so many of the blades. One of the advantages of a vertical turbine is that it doesn't care about wind direction, but those supports ruined some of that avantage.

I know the cause are just an experiment, but I really think you'll be better off spring the turbine on both ends of the axle. Higher wind loads won't affect it. They will affect this lazy Susan arrangement, because the castors opposite the wind will have higher loading since the axle is still a pivot point. In a true lazy Susan, there is no axle, no pivot, and no uneven loading. 

I do enjoy the experimentation, however!! Keep up the fine work. You've inspired my latest video on my channel about failure and troubleshooting.

author: @martincrabtree6704
text: The biggest problem facing wind power is that it's constantly turning so wears components out, if you saw what Siemens charge for a service on a wind powered gen you would not be saying 'awesome' that's for sure, it's eye wateringly expensive. I think you've created a failure point there. Those little castor wheels will be made to travel hundreds or thousands of miles at XXmph which will wear them out and also wear the surface they are running on, both quite quickly (even on a properly developed version with better components). The first idea was better IMO in that you are just running on two bearings which were designed (if you bought the right ones) to do tens of thousands of unmaintained miles at high load before needing changing (I'm pointing at a car wheel bearing here btw). You could go back to your last design and either turn the corner planks so they guide air in or simply ditch them in favour of 4 steel tubes with a length of threaded bar through the centre. If you chose the former the whole thing would need a vane on top and be mounted on another bearing, the wind would then spin the whole lot to face the correct way (over complicated perhaps?). At some point you're going to have to think of a way of slowing it down in high winds or even completely off (at night perhaps) otherwise some gale force will blow it to bits.  I know this is just a test to see what is achievable, but i'm thinking ahead here! Keep up the good work, you're one of a only few genuinley watchable people on YT.

author: @paulbrouyere1735
text: Thank you for the good laugh time and time again. I admire your creative mind, but here I think you’ld better choose the previous version with inclined stator vanes. (You had placed them at angles of 45 degrees, rotate that about 30 degrees and the results will be much better) the stator vanes should direct wind in the right direction. Even better would be to use curbed plates like you used in the rotor, but with an opposite curbing for the stator. Doing so you can harvest a larger windsurface and guide it towards the rotor, while blocking wind to blow on the wrong side of the rotor.

author: @lnwolf41
text: A good work around with the casters. you could of used angle iron as a frame work to steady the wheel. or just cut out out a "C" section from the wood, I am sure if you left a 2"  / 5 cm wide section all the way around it would quite sturdy.  for your coils, if you put 2-3 in parallel, then series with the next group, you might get a good balance of current and voltage. I know you would like to hook it directly to a load, but you would do better to use a charge controller to a battery, then a load. you would still produce power, but the battery will ensure your turbine isn't bogged down, just like the capacitor circuit you made.

author: @phillipr303
text: Suggestions:  Drop the casters and Replace your turntable with a simple donut ring base so the design still supports the blades on top and the magnets below this open ring.  Connect the  "center pole" to the new donut ring table with a new wireframe (like a bicycle wheel laying flat).  Find two magnets strong enough to create a  "magnetic support system" on the center pole.  The overall idea is to create a floating turntable. This eliminates any resistance and creates a Friction-Free system that is 1) lighter,  2) faster, and 3) more sensitive to any wind.  Would you please add a flag in the background when testing it outside so we can see what direction the wind is blowing?  (so I can see it)...  There are other design features that can be added to double the energy produced. I can email them later... IF, you would like?

author: @JimNichols
text:
In my humble opinion it is not a race to the finish, but a methodical step by step process designed to come to some usable fruition. Commentary otherwise is a product of the me, now generation, design that works is never now, but a combination of engineering, testing, well that didn't work well at all and re-design that ends up as a usable, durable product. 
Aeronautic engineering modeling is not my gig, nor is calculating laminar airflow over a flat surface, but it seems to the idiot in me that the uprights would be a great addition if they were angled to the same pitch as the turbine blades. Instead of the X configuration at 45 degrees to the corners of the base, maybe 20/70 degrees angled toward the pitch of the blades, anyway just my opinion. Great build, take the time to enjoy the process and the grey matter exercise and as always thanks for the time involved in the "filming" and editing these gems :)

author: @williamarmstrong7199
text:
Humm I would have used thin blades made out of teflon above a sheet of glass coated in a teflon sheet. So it acts like ice skates.
Perhaps a light spring loading to keep them in contact to the base would dampen any oscillations and help harmonic balance. Only maybe 1 or 2mm play between spring loading and full contact. I had thought about rubber damping but that changes too much between hot and cold. Perhaps some sort of air pressure between all the blades might work well if not too complex to create. Again temperature differences might cause problems a bent bit of spring steel or a spring might be the simplest and best option.

author: @TerryGilsenan
text:
There is a problem with casters and that is that as the speed of the turbine increases there will be a bending torque on the shaft that would load up the casters on the leeward side and lift them off the base plate on the windward side.
The centrifugal force  would then cause the castors when unloaded, to swing out on their own swivel bearing until they hit the ground plate and they would then have a braking force on the rotor as they were forced back into line with the central track that the castor swivels scribe around the axel.

This would also cause noise and knocking as the castors lift off and land in their rotation about the central axis. This would also throw the rotor out of balance as the windward side of the rotor would have pendulums sticking out as the casters swing out as the lift off the base plate.

And the action of swinging weight out from the central axis would have the effect of slowing the rotor down, like an ice skater using their arms to speed up and slow down a spin.

Suggestion: What about inverting the castors and screwing them to the base plate with the wheels running on the underside of the rotor? If the castors are stationary then the centrifugal force issue and the take off and landing issue is resolved. This will also reduce the “unsprung weight” of the rotor and its inertia.

Reminds me of the old and deadly  'witches hat' roundabouts of the 1970's.

What I am wondering about is any secondary force being applied to the rotor by any wind exiting the inside of the rotor. What makes me wonder about this is the design of overshot water wheels. They are designed so that the water exiting the wheel applies force. I am going to make a main comment about some of these ides. This thing will be up and running at maximum efficiency by 2030... ;0)

author: @jplater9191
text:
Hello Robert, a lot of mechanical drag comes with the castors. Fixating the axle at the top end by an external rigid frame will solve this. Magnetic bearings may help reduce friction drag as well. (Costly, i know)
If you look again at the leading fixed blades and apply both the lift formula and Bernouilli’s law to their angle of incidence you may come up with a ducted design that actually prevents the blades to stall. You can than experiment with varying angle of incidence according to the windspeed thus optimising the torque it gives. Finally it will help the efficiency greatly to adopt a simple 3d wing profile for all the blades.

author: @toyotaboyhatman
text: I love the concept of wind turbines, but to really be effective (because ground wind is very choppy) they need to be about 30 feet above a nearby roof.  I started looking a telescoping antenna mounts and I think it's feasible that an antenna mast could support the weight of a lightweight turbine.  They aren't terrible expensive, you just need to make sure they are well supported so they don't fall over in high winds.  Tension wires help a lot.

author: @BKroef
text:
Maybe you should put the bevels back on, but in a slightly different way. Looking from the top the construction would look like the outer lines of a swastika or solarwheel. So the blades running against the wind direction are being protected against that wind. The blades running in the wind direction will be hit by the wind. Also there will be an exit of the wind because of a little pressure build up in the vawt. That wind will get sucked out of the vawt and also push against the blades where it leaves the vawt.
Although, that was my thoughts on it when I was experimenting with them.

author: @target070
text: I think the inherent weakness of this design is that the wind is always hitting both sides of the turbine, or 180 degrees. Example: the blower you used in your shop to spin the turbine was directed to hit only the scoop side, say 15 degrees of the turbine, with no headwind on the leeward side. You had maximum propulsion without any extra drag. However outside, say the wind is blowing from the west, it hits 1/2 of the wheel all the time, so the only net gain is the windward vs leeward vein angle efficiency. I wonder if you could assemble a free spinning half or quarter cover with a large tail fin, that would rotate with the headwind to always cover the leeward veins. If you made a cage that fits over the wheel, that could rotate freely around it directed by a large tail fin, similar to a traditional windmill, which would turn the cage/cover with the headwind. Using your turbine as an example, it would have a shield or cover, to block the headwind to the left front quadrant of turbine. Depending on the design or complexity of the shield, It could be made to funnel the wind TO the scoop side of the blades, maybe like a velocity stack.

replies.author: @goldmagnet9013
replies.text: Totally agree, but if you make this modification then why go with a VAWT at all ? The main advantage of a VAWT is that they can instantly harness wind from any direction without having to turn into the wind, with this mod they lose that advantage so you would just go for a HAWT, which are way more efficient.

replies.author: @stupot8413
replies.text: That was one of my thoughts too. 👍

replies.author: @goldmagnet9013
replies.text: @stupot8413 Yes and Robs quoting cut in speeds with no magnets and coils.  When I made my blades HAWT I tested them running on just a bearing, and they would turn when you could not feel the wind. In the wind mill world "cut in" refers to the speed the turbine start charging a battery, the speed they start turning is "start up".  All in all 1.8 ms is not a great start up speed for a turbine with no generator, my guess the increased friction is causing the issue..

replies.author: @target070
replies.text: @goldmagnet9013  I don't disagree, but going with Robert's theme of using what you already have and experimenting with it, plus  his idea to try outside circumference magnets instead of the typical center design, I'm just wondering if this would make it more efficient.

author: @johnmcginnis5201
text: Does simplify the build a great deal. I would however steal an idea I see in many microwave ovens. A circular rim with axle studs to which skateboard bearings are attached. The bearing ride in a circular track in the base to maintain alignment. The top of the bearings ride ride against the base of the mill. (the glass plate in a microwave.) Maintains rock solid vertical orientation.

author: @tg_privat
text: Have you tryed adjusting the angle of the blades. They are in fact wings. They have maximal lyft at a specific angle to the direction of the wind. And max lyft direction should be tangential to the cyrcle as much as possible. So you get maximal momentum. Looking at your turbine, I would say, this is not exactly the case.

author: @shanealexander9952
text:
I saw that also but didn't say it...I was debating 1" conduit for framework or non metal pvc with sand and a 4way fitting at the top.  Only if you find the casters a problem.
As you show it now, it occurs to me that there are also other fluid dynamics.  There is now also a small internal pressure of air coming inside  but out in all directions.  there is also a slight counter push from wind direction.  I call the both adding up to 0.  I would ignore that mess and press on.  
But if I can ever make mine, I want to incorporate a freewheeling half side baffle on the non push side with a fin downwind to keep the baffle orientated.  Air exiting the non push side would get channeled out downwind or blocked (not sure which to do).  or free articulating the fins slightly. An unnecessary nuance of mine.  That would be Post experimenting at the stage it is at now.  I wouldn't have imagined anything at all if you had not built yours. You keep me thinking.

author: @dremaboy777
text:
Hmmm...
I know you say the wheels ate barely touching, but... All I see is friction and drag issues. I could be wrong of course.

I'm thinking, a  L-shape aluminum bar (say 1-1/2" X 1-1/2") attached also in a L shape from the side and to the central point on top from two sides (or four), might be a better option. 
And if you think of something both lightweight and sturdy... then... just one L-shaped Attachment would deal with any wobble issues. 

Maybe it's just me but, the friction and drag I'm assuming because of those wheels... rubs me like nails across a chalk board 😂.

And honestly... It seemed to be rotating slower to me. I don't think that baffles are better (unless on the right angle), but I think the wheels are worse. 

So... One solid, lightweight 
"L-beam" to the top. 

Just my 2¢

author: @nunyabiznez8120
text: While the casters are a pretty good idea, using piping to make the roof to keep it square would work and minimally interfere with the air flow.  The casters are most likely the plastic bushing type that will wear out under load fairly quickly.

replies.author: @goldmagnet9013
replies.text: @James Spinning at enough RPM to generate meaningful power on a windy day they will be under significant load. Because the load from the wind is different on each side, the forces create significant vertical load. Its the main failure point on pole mounted VAWTs.
